items.evidence_text:
Client 1: Works for me.
Advisor: Great. Michael, Elizabeth—what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The household gross income is 114300 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, that includes employment plus any other income streams you have?
Client 2: Yes. We both work, and then there’s some other stuff—like rental income and then interest and dividends.

Client 2: It’s around nine thousand a month… like nine-ish.
Advisor: Okay—when you say nine-ish, do you have a number you use when you budget? Even if it’s not perfect.
Client 2: Yes—9050 per month is what we’ve been using.
Advisor: Perfect. Now within that, I want to separate out debt payments. What are your monthly debt payments total—so things like the mortgage, any loans, anything like that?
Client 1: Just the mortgage. Monthly debt payments total is 3550.

Client 2: Yes—9050 per month is what we’ve been using.
Advisor: Perfect. Now within that, I want to separate out debt payments. What are your monthly debt payments total—so things like the mortgage, any loans, anything like that?
Client 1: Just the mortgage. Monthly debt payments total is 3550.
Advisor: Thanks. And that mortgage—do you remember the balance and rate offhand, or should we come back to it?
Client 1: I’ve got it. Balance outstanding is $178,800 and the interest rate is 6.25%. The final payment date is 2030-10-15.

Client 2: Right, no other loans.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s switch to what you own—investments and accounts first, then the home. Roughly, what’s the total you’d call investable assets across everything you can invest?
Client 1: Investable assets total is 379700.
Advisor: Okay. Now I’ll ask a couple quick “what and where” questions so I understand what makes up that total. Michael, do you have a taxable account—like a brokerage—held through an insurance company?
Client 1: Yes. I have a taxable account held at an insurance company, and it’s valued at $231,750.

Client 2: Yes, we have a joint account on an advisor platform—value is $26,500.
Advisor: Perfect. And then the home—your primary residence—what value are you using for that?
Client 1: We use $325,850 for the primary residence value.
Advisor: Thanks. That helps me connect the dots: investments, the house, and the mortgage. Before we wrap this section—do either of you have any life insurance or disability coverage we should factor in, or is that still a question mark?
Client 2: We don’t have anything set up right now. It’s kind of on the list.

Client 1: Just the mortgage. Monthly debt payments total is 3550.
Advisor: Thanks. And that mortgage—do you remember the balance and rate offhand, or should we come back to it?
Client 1: I’ve got it. Balance outstanding is $178,800 and the interest rate is 6.25%. The final payment date is 2030-10-15.
Advisor: Great detail—thank you. I’m just going to note that down. No other debts besides that mortgage, right?
Client 2: Right, no other loans.

Client 1: Just the mortgage. Monthly debt payments total is 3550.
Advisor: Thanks. And that mortgage—do you remember the balance and rate offhand, or should we come back to it?
Client 1: I’ve got it. Balance outstanding is $178,800 and the interest rate is 6.25%. The final payment date is 2030-10-15.
Advisor: Great detail—thank you. I’m just going to note that down. No other debts besides that mortgage, right?
Client 2: Right, no other loans.

Client 1: Works for me.
Advisor: Great. Michael, Elizabeth—what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The household gross income is 114300 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, that includes employment plus any other income streams you have?
Client 2: Yes. We both work, and then there’s some other stuff—like rental income and then interest and dividends.

Client 1: Yeah, exactly—college for the two kids, plus retirement.
Advisor: Makes sense. Switching gears for one minute—taxes. Michael, what bracket do you typically think you’re in right now? Just the band you plan around.
Client 1: We’re in the 37% bracket.
Advisor: Okay, thanks. That helps me not recommend something that looks good pre-tax but stings after-tax.
Client 2: Yeah, taxes are… not fun.

Advisor: Hi Michael, hi Elizabeth—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this feel like a good first meeting for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I want it organized—like, let’s get the facts straight and then talk options. The mortgage is the big fixed thing for us.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… a plan that doesn’t feel super restrictive month to month.
Advisor: Totally. Why don’t we start high level with cash flow, then we’ll map out what you own and what you owe, and we’ll circle back to protection like life insurance after that. Sound okay?

Advisor: Fair. And just to keep the planning assumptions consistent—are we doing this plan based in the US?
Client 2: Yes, US.
Advisor: Perfect. And last admin-ish question, then we’ll go right back to goals: when you think about how you want advice delivered, are you looking for an ongoing relationship where I’m acting as your fiduciary—so, an RIA, registered investment advisor—or more of a one-time plan?
Client 1: Ongoing. RIA (registered investment advisor).
Advisor: Great—thank you. Let me jot that down. Okay, back to goals: for college, are you thinking you want to cover, like, a portion versus the full ride for both kids?

Advisor: Hi Michael, hi Elizabeth—nice to meet you both. I’m glad you made the time. Before we get into numbers, what would make this feel like a good first meeting for you?
Client 1: Thanks. I want it organized—like, let’s get the facts straight and then talk options. The mortgage is the big fixed thing for us.
Client 2: Yeah, and just… a plan that doesn’t feel super restrictive month to month.
Advisor: Totally. Why don’t we start high level with cash flow, then we’ll map out what you own and what you owe, and we’ll circle back to protection like life insurance after that. Sound okay?
Client 1: Works for me.

Advisor: First quick one—it's just the two of you as adults in the household, correct?
Client 1: Yes—2 adults.
Advisor: Thanks. And you’re married, right?
Client 2: Yes, we’re married.
Advisor: Great. For planning assumptions, what state are you resident in right now?

Advisor: Great. Michael, Elizabeth—what do you consider your household gross income for the year, all sources combined?
Client 1: The household gross income is 114300 annually.
Advisor: Got it—thank you. And just so I’m not missing anything, that includes employment plus any other income streams you have?
Client 2: Yes. We both work, and then there’s some other stuff—like rental income and then interest and dividends.
Advisor: Okay. Let’s pin down spending next. About how much are your total monthly expenses, all-in—housing, childcare, groceries, everything?

Client 1: It’s listed as Other.
Advisor: Okay, thank you—we’ll keep state tax assumptions conservative until we can clarify that later.
Advisor: Now, on risk comfort—when markets swing, would you say conservative, moderate, or aggressive fits best?
Client 2: I’d say moderate. Like, I can handle some ups and downs, but I don’t want it to feel scary.
Advisor: Got it. Michael, would you put yourself in that same camp?

Advisor: On income: Michael, I have employment income of $83,900 annually, plus rental income of $42,900 annually that comes in kind of ad hoc—still accurate?
Client 1: Yes—$83,900 from my job, and rental income is $42,900 annually, and it’s ad hoc.
Advisor: And Elizabeth, I have your employment income at $30,400 annually, and interest and dividends at $71,400 annually, paid weekly—does that match?
Client 2: Yes. My job is $30,400 a year, and the interest and dividends are $71,400 annually, and it hits weekly.
Advisor: Great—thank you. Let’s go to assets. Michael, you have a taxable account held through an insurance company at $231,750—right?

Client 1: Yep, I’m Client 1.
Client 2: And I’m Client 2, yes.
Advisor: Perfect. Elizabeth, just rounding out your profile—what’s your role in the household? Like spouse or partner, that kind of thing.
Client 2: I’m the spouse/partner.
Advisor: Got it. And you’re currently working—are you employed, self-employed, on leave, anything like that?

Client 2: Employed.
Advisor: Okay. And what do you do day-to-day—what’s your occupation area? I’m not looking for a fancy title, more like the lane you’re in.
Client 2: Operations. Like, operations support and coordination stuff.
Advisor: Helpful—thank you. And just to anchor the income piece again, Elizabeth—your gross annual income from your job is 30400, correct?
Client 2: Yes—30400 gross annually from my job.

Advisor: Perfect. Elizabeth, just rounding out your profile—what’s your role in the household? Like spouse or partner, that kind of thing.
Client 2: I’m the spouse/partner.

Client 2: Employed.
